question.text: Partly paid-up units in an AIF represent:

answer.text: The portion of committed capital that has been invested (drawn down) by the investor in the AIF
Units held only by the sponsor

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Partly paid-up units represent the portion of committed capital that has been actually invested (drawn down) by the investor. Since AIFs call capital progressively through drawdowns, units start as partly paid and become fully paid as all committed capital is drawn. **The Trap:** Partly paid doesn't mean the units are worth less — it means the full commitment hasn't been called yet. The investor's remaining commitment will be called through future drawdown notices. **Cross-Exam Context:** Unit capital concepts including partly paid units are tested in XIX-A. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Help investors understand that partly paid units reflect the drawdown stage of their investment. Their total commitment remains obligatory even though only a portion has been called. They should keep liquid reserves available for future drawdowns.
